3-Day Intensive Course

Agentic AI
for Developers

From LLM completions to production-grade multi-agent systems.

3
Days
9
Sessions
6
Topologies
5
Sens-AI Habits

Day One

LLM Based Agents

From stateless completions to autonomous reasoning loops — agent fundamentals, frameworks, tools, ReAct, planning, and RAG.

What is an Agent? LangGraph Ollama ReAct Plan & Execute RAG + Ragas

Session 1.1

LLM vs Agent

LLM (completion)
Input → Model → Output Stateless. One shot. Forgets everything. You send a prompt, it generates text.
Agent
Perceive → Reason → Act → Observe → ↻ Stateful loop. Has tools. Accumulates context. Decides what to do next.
The PRAO Loop

Perception → Reasoning → Action → Observation — the agent repeats this until done or stopped.

Session 1.1 · The Differentiator

The PRAO Loop — what turns an LLM into an Agent

LLM alone = just R

One Reasoning step. No senses, no hands, no feedback. prompt → text and it's over. Cannot check its own work.

Agent = LLM + Loop + Tools

Same LLM, now embedded in P→R→A→O. It can fetch data, call tools, see results, and retry — adapting on every turn.

Session 1.1 · Foundations

Classic Agent Types Russell & Norvig · AIMA

  • Simple Reflex — maps current percept directly to action via condition–action rules. No memory, no world model. Works only in fully observable environments. e.g. thermostat, spam filter.
  • Model-Based Reflex — keeps internal state to track what it cannot currently see. A world model fills gaps in perception. e.g. vacuum robot remembering cleaned rooms.
  • Goal-Based — acts to reach explicit goals. Considers future consequences. Search and planning emerge here. e.g. GPS routing, chess engine.
  • Utility-Based — picks the action with highest expected utility. Trades off competing goals — speed vs cost vs safety. e.g. ride-share dispatcher, autonomous driving.
  • Learning — improves over time via a critic, learning element, and problem generator. Any of the above can become a learning agent. e.g. AlphaGo, RLHF-tuned LLMs.

Session 1.1 · Architecture

Simple Reflex Agent

Key idea

A lookup table from percept to action. The fastest possible agent — and the most fragile. Loops forever if the same percept yields the wrong action.

Real example

Thermostat, regex-based spam filter, deterministic form validator, rate-limit middleware.

Session 1.1 · Architecture

Model-Based Reflex Agent

Key idea

Adds an internal world model so the agent can act in partially observable environments. State is updated each tick from the percept and the last action.

Real example

Roomba mapping cleaned rooms, conversational chatbot with short-term memory, TCP retransmit logic.

Session 1.1 · Architecture

Goal-Based Agent

Key idea

Action chosen by simulating outcomes against an explicit goal. Search and planning live here. Slower than reflex — but flexible when goals change.

Real example

GPS routing, chess engine minimax, build-system dependency resolver, plan-and-execute LLM agents.

Session 1.1 · Architecture

Utility-Based Agent

Key idea

Generalises goals to a continuous utility function. Trades off competing objectives — speed, cost, safety — and handles uncertainty via expected utility.

Real example

Ride-share dispatcher, autonomous-driving cost map, LLM-judge model routing (cheap vs accurate vs fast).

Session 1.1 · Architecture

Learning Agent

Key idea

A meta-architecture that wraps any of the prior four. The Critic compares behaviour to a fixed standard; the Learning Element edits the Performance Element accordingly.

Real example

AlphaGo (self-play), RLHF-tuned LLMs, eval-driven prompt tuning, online recommender systems with bandit exploration.

Session 1.1 · Foundations

The Capability Ladder

Reflex + state Model-Based + goals Goal-Based + preferences Utility-Based + adaptation Learning
Each type adds one capability the previous one lacked. Pick the simplest type that solves the problem.
Why this still matters

The lens is universal. An LLM agent is, in classic terms, a learning utility-based agent where language is both percept and action.

The cardinal sin

Reaching for "learning" or "multi-agent" when a reflex rule would do. Complexity must be earned, not assumed.

Session 1.1 · Foundations

Classic Theory → LLM Era

Classic typeLLM-era manifestationExample
Simple ReflexStateless prompt → completionInline code suggestion
Model-Based ReflexConversation memory + tool callsReAct loop, Claude Code
Goal-BasedPlan-and-execute agentsDevin, AutoGPT
Utility-BasedCost / latency / quality routingModel routing, judge-based selection
LearningRLHF, in-context learning, eval-driven loopsRLHF-tuned models, prompt-tuning harnesses
PEAS still applies

Before building, specify Performance measure, Environment, Actuators (tools), Sensors (inputs). The vocabulary is 30 years old and still correct.

Session 1.2

Framework Landscape

FrameworkStrengthUse When
LangGraph ★Explicit state graphs, checkpoints, HITLProduction agents needing control flow
CrewAIDeclarative role-based teamsQuick prototypes, role-driven workflows
AutoGenMulti-agent natural language turnsResearch, debate, collaborative tasks
LlamaIndexEvent-driven Workflows, 300+ data connectors, best-in-class RAG & indexingData/RAG-first agents — doc Q&A, knowledge bases, structured extraction
Google ADKMulti-language (Py/TS/Go/Java), Session/State/Memory, A2A nativeProduction agents on Vertex AI / Gemini Enterprise
AWS StrandsModel-driven loop, MCP-native, OTel observabilityLean agents on AWS (Lambda, Bedrock AgentCore)
Hosted: Claude API Local: Ollama (llama3.2, qwen2.5-coder)

Session 1.2

Tool Design Principles

  • Precise names — query_database not query. The name is the model's primary routing signal.
  • Docstring as contract — describe what it does, each arg, what it returns, and hard constraints.
  • Structured returns — return dicts, not raw strings. The model reasons about structure better than prose.
  • Validate up front — reject bad input immediately with a clear message. The model will read it and self-correct.
  • Scope constraints — never give an agent more access than it needs. Least privilege applies.

Session 1.2

ReAct Agent

User message → LLM reasons → Tool call → Observation → LLM reasons → Final answer
Repeat until no tool calls (end_turn) or MAX_ITERATIONS reached.
Max Iterations

Always set a hard cap (e.g. 15). Without it, the agent loops forever when stuck. Track iteration_count in state.

Error Classes

Hallucinated tool — explain & list valid names.
Policy block — block, do not retry.
Transient error — explain, let agent retry.

Session 1.3

Plan & Execute

ReAct
Figures out the next step only after seeing the previous result. Reactive. Good for exploratory tasks.
Plan & Execute
Generates full plan before first action. Replans on failure. Separates strategic thinking from execution.
The split

Planner (claude-sonnet, called once) → decomposes into 3–7 steps.
Executor (claude-haiku + tools, called per step) → mini ReAct loop per step.
Replanner → triggered on step failure, generates new plan from that point.

Session 1.3

RAG for Agents

Query → Embed → Vector search → Top-K chunks → Inject → LLM
In agents: retrieval is a tool — the agent decides when to call it, what to search, and how many results.
Knowledge Graph

Stores entities + relationships. Enables structured traversal queries. Complements vector search where relationships matter.

Ragas Evaluation

Faithfulness — hallucination detection.
Context Precision — retrieval relevance.
Context Recall — retrieval completeness.
Answer Relevancy — response quality.

Session 1.3 · Evaluation

Ragas — How RAG Evaluation Actually Works

Ship it · put it in CI

result = evaluate(dataset, metrics=[faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_precision, context_recall]) — three metrics need no ground truth, so you can run them on live traffic. Fail the build when Faithfulness drops below your threshold; the hallucination never ships.

Session 1.3 · Knowledge Graph

When Vector RAG Hits a Wall

The fundamental difference

Vector RAG retrieves what is similar. Knowledge Graphs retrieve what is related. Multi-hop queries — "X of Y of Z" — are where similarity quietly fails and relations win.

Session 1.3 · Knowledge Graph

Six Wins for Knowledge Graphs

DimensionVector RAGKnowledge Graph
Multi-hop reasoningBrittle — synthesis across chunksNative — graph traversal
ExplainabilityOpaque cosine scoresThe path is the answer
DeterminismEmbedding drift between modelsExact traversal — same query, same path
SchemaNone — free textTyped entities & relations
AggregationsVery limitedCount, group, filter, shortest-path
Phantom entitiesHallucination riskOnly existing nodes can be returned
Best-fit domains

Org charts, supply chains, compliance & regulation, code dependencies, drug interactions, fraud rings — anywhere relations are the data.

The trade-off

Index-time cost rises — entity extraction (LLM-driven) and schema discipline are required. Setup days, not minutes.

Session 1.3 · Knowledge Graph

GraphRAG — The Hybrid Pattern

Use both when

Relational queries on top of an unstructured corpus, audit-required answers, "list / count / who-related-to-whom" questions over enterprise knowledge.

Stick with vector RAG when

Pure semantic search, long-form prose Q&A, fast iteration, no clear entity schema. Don't pay the GraphRAG tax for similarity-only problems.

Session 1.3 · Chunking

Why Chunking Matters

Too small

Snippets ripped from context. Retrieval finds the right line but the LLM has nothing to reason with.

Too large

Multiple topics averaged into one embedding vector. Cosine similarity drops, retrieval misses, irrelevant text fills the context window.

Wrong boundary

Cuts mid-sentence or mid-code-block. Embedding distorted, LLM sees grammatically broken text.

Session 1.3 · Chunking · Strategy 1

Fixed-Size + Overlap

When to use

Plain unstructured text, demos, baseline benchmarks. Fast and predictable — sometimes "good enough" is the right answer.

Watch out for

Cuts code blocks, tables, JSON, and Markdown headings in half. Storage cost grows linearly with overlap percentage.

Session 1.3 · Chunking · Strategy 2

Recursive Character Splitting

When to use

Default for prose: docs, articles, blog posts, support tickets. Respects natural boundaries opportunistically — almost always beats fixed-size.

Watch out for

Default separator list is English-centric. Code, JSON, and structured formats need a custom list — Language.PYTHON, Language.MARKDOWN.

Session 1.3 · Chunking · Strategy 3

Document-Aware Chunking

When to use

Markdown, HTML, source code, RST, JSON. Anywhere structure exists, exploit it — the boundaries were already labelled by the author.

Watch out for

Sections vary wildly in size — combine with a size-based splitter inside each section. Useless on plain prose.

Session 1.3 · Chunking · Strategy 4

Semantic Chunking

When to use

Long-form technical writing where topics shift mid-section: research papers, transcripts, mixed-topic blog posts. Quality over speed.

Watch out for

Slow — embeds every sentence at index time. Threshold tuning required. Falls back to recursive splitting if all sentences look similar.

Session 1.3 · Chunking · Strategy 5

Parent-Child & What's Next

When to use

When small chunks improve retrieval precision but the LLM needs surrounding context. ParentDocumentRetriever (LangChain), SmallToBigRetriever (LlamaIndex).

Advanced (2024+)

Contextual Retrieval (Anthropic) — prepend an LLM-generated context blurb to each chunk; ~49% retrieval-failure reduction.
Late Chunking (Jina AI) — embed the whole doc with a long-context model, then chunk the embeddings.

Day Two

Single Agent Mastery

AI-First development mindset, Sens-AI habits, MCP, context engineering, four memory types, observability, and guardrails.

Sens-AI Framework MCP Context Engineering 4 Memory Types Langfuse Guardrails

Session 2.1

AI-First Software Development

The rehash loop — key failure mode

AI keeps generating slight variations of the same wrong answer. It's not a prompt problem — the AI has exhausted the context you gave it. Stop prompting. Apply the Sens-AI habits.

Traditional dev

Developer writes code → runs tests → ships.

AI-First dev

Developer provides strategic intent + framing + judgment. Agent handles mechanical execution.

Session 2.1 · Sens-AI Framework

Five Habits Andrew Stellman · O'Reilly 2025

Context → Research → Framing → Refining → Critical Thinking
Critical Thinking reveals what Context was missing — the loop restarts, compounding quality over time.
01
Context

What does AI need to know?

02
Research

Deepen your own understanding

03
Framing

Define what you're solving

04
Refining

Iterate deliberately

05
Critical Thinking

Judge before you trust

Session 2.1 · Sens-AI Framework

Habits in Practice

#HabitThe question to askKey anti-pattern
01 ContextSupply code, intent, constraints, what you've tried"What does AI need that it doesn't have?"Thin prompt: "fix the bug"
02 ResearchUse AI + docs + codebase to you understand better"Do I understand this enough to guide AI?"Prompting out of a stuck loop
03 FramingWhat you want + constraints + don'ts + definition of done"Am I solving the right problem, precisely?"Vague: "make it more testable"
04 RefiningRead → diagnose specifically → one targeted change"What was wrong, and what one thing do I change?"Random tweaks → vibe coding
05 CriticalGenerate alternatives, use AI as its own critic, 6-month test"Is this correct and will it hold up?"Accepting first output passively

Session 2.1

Rules, Commands & Skills

Rules
Operating Constraints

Defined in CLAUDE.md. Role, capabilities, constraints, escalation rules. Loaded every session. Applies Sens-AI Context habit upfront.

Commands
Predefined Workflows

Slash commands: /review /test /migrate /doc. Pre-engineered prompts for recurring tasks. Applies Sens-AI Framing habit.

Skills
Reusable Sub-procedures

Self-contained agent workflows with typed input/output contracts. Include a self-evaluation step. Applies Sens-AI Critical Thinking habit.

Sub-agents: Local vs Cloud

Local — in-process LangGraph graph, zero network, shared memory.
Cloud — HTTP delegation to a deployed agent; returns when complete.

Session 2.1

Model Context Protocol (MCP)

What it solves

Before MCP: every agent needed custom integration code per tool. After MCP: write a tool server once, any compatible agent uses it — including Claude Code.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ MCP Client (Agent) MCP Server (Tool Provider) │ │ ─────────────────── ───────────────────────── │ │ Claude Code ←──→ Filesystem server │ │ Custom LangGraph ←──→ GitHub server │ │ Any MCP client ←──→ PostgreSQL server │ │ ←──→ Your custom server │ │ │ │ JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio / HTTP+SSE │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Session 2.1 · MCP Architecture

Three Roles, One Protocol

HOST

The AI app the user runs. Owns the LLM, UI, consent, and one or more clients.

CLIENT

Lives inside the host. Holds a single 1:1 connection to one server. Speaks JSON-RPC.

SERVER

Standalone process. Exposes tools, resources, prompts. Local or remote.

Session 2.1 · The Host

MCP Host — the AI App You Use

Definition

The user-facing AI application that drives the conversation, calls the LLM, and orchestrates one or more MCP clients. It owns the trust boundary — every tool call passes through the host's consent layer before reaching a server.

5 Responsibilities

1. Spawn & supervise client instances
2. Aggregate tools/resources from all servers
3. Route LLM tool calls → correct client
4. Consent — approve / deny per invocation
5. UI — chat, file picker, auth flows

Hosts in the wild

Claude Desktop / Code — Anthropic reference
Cursor · Windsurf · Zed — IDE-native
Continue · Cline — open-source
ChatGPT Desktop — OpenAI
Your LangGraph agent — DIY w/ adapter

Mental model

Host = the browser tab. LLM = the engine inside it. Each MCP client = a fetch connection (one per origin). Servers = the websites being fetched.

Session 2.1 · The Client

MCP Client — One Wire per Server

What it is

An in-process module of the host that owns exactly one stateful connection to exactly one server. N servers ⇒ N clients.

Transports

stdio — host spawns server as subprocess; talks via stdin/stdout. Local, zero-config.
streamable HTTP — POST + SSE for remote servers. Auth via OAuth 2.

Session 2.1 · The Server

MCP Server — Three Capability Types

Tools
Executable functions

Model-invoked actions with side effects. The LLM picks a tool, the host gates with consent, the server runs it.

write_file · run_query · send_email

Resources
Read-only data sources

Application-controlled context the host loads for the LLM — files, schemas, log streams. Addressed by URI.

file://README.md · postgres://schema

Prompts
User-triggered templates

Pre-baked workflows the user picks from a slash-menu. Server fills variables, returns a ready-to-send message.

/summarize_pr · /explain_query

The deal

Wrap any system once as an MCP server — local CLI, internal API, vector DB — and every MCP host can use it without bespoke glue. The integration matrix collapses from N × M to N + M.

Session 2.2

Context Engineering

Token budget for a 100K context agent: ────────────────────────────────────────── 4,000 → System prompt + rules (required, never trimmed) 10,000 → Retrieved memory (priority 8) 30,000 → Conversation history (priority 6) 20,000 → Tool results (priority 5) 20,000 → Current document / code (priority 4) 16,000 → Reserved for model output ──────────────────────────────────────────
  • Lost-in-the-middle — LLMs miss info in the middle. Put critical instructions at the top.
  • Context poisoning — stale/wrong tool results accumulate and mislead. Tag errors, strip irrelevant history.
  • Priority trimming — when budget overflows, trim lowest-priority slots first. System prompt is sacred.

Session 2.2

Four Memory Types

Session
Working Memory

LangGraph AgentState. Current conversation only. O(1) access. Lost when session ends.

Episodic
Past Run Logs

ChromaDB + embeddings. "Last time I fixed auth errors, I did X." Written after each run, retrieved at session start.

Semantic
Domain Knowledge

Vector DB (Chroma/Qdrant). API docs, codebase patterns. Retrieved by similarity per query.

Procedural
How-To Knowledge

CLAUDE.md / system prompt snippets. Heuristics, workflows, coding standards. Loaded at startup.

Session 2.3

Observability with Langfuse

Why agents need tracing

When an agent produces a wrong answer: which LLM call failed? What was the exact prompt? Which tool was called with what args? How much did it cost? Without traces, you're debugging blind.

Langfuse captures automatically

Full trace tree · LLM spans (prompt, response, tokens) · Tool spans (name, args, output) · Cost per run · Latency per step

Operational metrics to alert on

Tool error rate >40% → alert
Run duration >120s → alert
Token cost per run
LLM calls per task

Session 2.3

Three Guardrail Layers

User Input ──→ [INPUT GUARDRAIL] ──→ Agent │ Action planned (tool call) │ [TOOL CALL GUARDRAIL] ──→ Tools │ Tool result received │ [OUTPUT GUARDRAIL] ──→ User Response
Input

Screen for prompt injection, jailbreaks, harmful requests. Wrap external content in [EXTERNAL_CONTENT] tags.

Tool Call

Policy check before execution. Block deletes, env files, production deploys. Pause + human approval for irreversible actions.

Output

Screen for leaked secrets, PII (email, SSN, API keys), internal system details. Redact before returning to user.

Day Three

Multi-Agent Systems

Six topology patterns, agent-to-agent protocols (A2A, ACP), evaluation frameworks, and capstone project.

6 Topologies A2A Protocol ACP Protocol Tool Call Accuracy Trajectory Eval Capstone

Session 3.1

When to Use Multi-Agent

Reach for it when
Task exceeds single context window
Subtasks are genuinely parallel
Different parts need different expertise
Need redundancy / consensus
Do NOT reach for it when
A single agent with good tools can handle it
You're still debugging the single-agent version
Coordination overhead > the benefit
Rule of thumb

Every agent you add is a new failure point. Start simple. Only reach for multi-agent when the single-agent ceiling genuinely limits you.

Session 3.1

Six Topology Patterns

Supervisor → Worker
Supervisor decomposes & dispatches to specialists in parallel.
Use when: parallel, independent subtasks
Router → Expert
Router classifies request, routes to the right specialist.
Use when: many request types, clear categories
Orchestrator
Dynamically decides which agent to call next based on intermediate results.
Use when: dynamic, adaptive workflows
Round Table
All agents see the same task; moderator synthesises consensus.
Use when: diverse perspectives needed
Tree of Agents
Recursive decomposition; results propagate back up the tree.
Use when: massive tasks, recursive structure
Pipeline
Linear stages: each agent's output is the next agent's input.
Use when: ordered stages, deterministic flow

Session 3.2

A2A Protocol Google, 2025

Client Agent Remote Agent ───────────── ──────────── GET /.well-known/agent.json → Agent Card (name, skills, endpoint) POST /tasks → Task accepted (returns task_id) GET /tasks/{id}/stream → SSE: working → completed + result
Agent Card

JSON manifest: name, version, skills, capabilities. Enables discovery. Any A2A client can use any A2A server without custom code.

ACP (IBM)

Async message bus (Redis Streams/pub-sub). Better for long-running pipelines with many agents. A2A = request/response; ACP = event-driven.

Session 3.2

Agent Evaluation

Tool Call Accuracy

Was the right tool called with the right arguments?

Checks: correct tool selected · argument match · hallucinated tool names · unnecessary calls

Trajectory Evaluation

Was the sequence of tool calls efficient?

Metrics: efficiency ratio = reference_steps / actual_steps · loop count · backtrack count

Eval-driven development

Write eval cases before implementing features. When a production bug occurs, immediately translate it into a new test case. Run evals in CI on every PR touching a prompt or tool.

Session 3.3

Capstone Project

Option A
Autonomous PR Reviewer

Router topology. Security + Performance + Style workers. Human approval for blocking reviews. Langfuse traces.

Option B
Incident Responder

Triage classifier + automated remediation. Knowledge graph of known incidents. Escalate only on unknown failures.

Option C
Research Synthesiser

Pipeline topology. Web + internal docs retrieval. Cited reports. Ragas faithfulness ≥0.85. Self-curating memory.

Deliverables

Agent graph diagram · Memory design table · Tool inventory with safety class · 10 eval cases · Top 3 failure modes + mitigations

Day Three · Extended

Agent Performance
Engineering

Four levers that turn a working agent into a production-ready one: prompt caching, semantic caching, right-sized models, and async parallelism.

Prompt Caching Semantic Cache Model Routing Async Parallel Token Budgets

Session 3.4 · Extended

The Performance Tax

10-step ReAct agent · 5,000-token system prompt Per call: 5,000 tokens × $3/MTok = $0.015 10 calls: $0.015 × 10 = $0.15 / task 1,000/day: $0.15 × 1,000 = $150 / day ← just input tokens Multi-agent (5 agents × 3,000 tokens × 5 steps): 75,000 input tokens before a single output token
Four Levers

Prompt Caching — 90% off repeated system prompts
Semantic Cache — skip the LLM for similar queries
Model Routing — Haiku for simple, Opus for hard
Async Parallel — N steps in 1× latency, not N×

The Compound Cost Trap

Each agent in a multi-agent pipeline re-sends its own system prompt on every step. 5 agents × large system prompts = 5× the baseline cost before any work is done.

Session 3.4 · Extended

Prompt Caching Anthropic / OpenAI

Anthropic — Explicit
Mark blocks with cache_control: ephemeral
Cache write: 125% of normal price
Cache read: 10% of normal price (90% off)
TTL: 5 min, resets on each hit
Min: 1,024 tokens (Sonnet/Haiku)
OpenAI — Automatic
No code changes required
Stable prefix ≥ 1,024 tokens is eligible
Cache hit: 50% off input tokens
Shown in prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens
Structure rule

Stable content first (system prompt, tool defs, domain knowledge). Dynamic content last (user message, date, session ID). Reversing this order breaks the cache on every call.

Session 3.4 · Extended

Right-Sized Models

Haiku 4.5 — Fast

Classification · Extraction · Routing decisions · Yes/no guards · JSON parsing · Simple Q&A

~20× cheaper than Opus

Sonnet 4.6 — Balanced

Code generation · Summarisation · Multi-step reasoning · Tool orchestration · RAG synthesis

Default for agentic tasks

Opus 4.6 — Powerful

Complex planning · Cross-document reasoning · Research synthesis · Nuanced judgment

Use sparingly

RouteLLM (Stanford)

Learned routing classifier trained on your data. Consistently achieves 2–4× cost reduction with <5% quality loss. Source: github.com/lm-sys/RouteLLM

Session 3.4 · Extended

Semantic Cache & Async Parallelism

Semantic Caching

Store LLM responses, serve similar queries from cache — zero LLM calls for cache hits.

LangChain: InMemorySemanticCache / RedisSemanticCache
threshold=0.95 → conservative (near-exact)
threshold=0.85 → aggressive (similar queries)

⚠ Use TTLs. Stale answers are wrong answers.

Async Parallelism

Sequential N steps = N× latency.
Parallel N steps ≈ 1× latency.

asyncio.gather(*tasks) — fan-out multiple LLM calls
LangGraph Send API — parallel nodes with fan-in
asyncio.Semaphore(5) — cap concurrency to avoid rate limits

5 docs @ 1.5s each: 7.5s → 1.7s

Lab 3.4 — Performance Audit

Instrument your capstone: log token usage per call · enable prompt caching · add model routing · parallelise tool calls. Target: ≥40% token cost reduction · ≥30% p95 latency reduction.

End of Course

What You Can
Build Now

TimelineWhat to buildApplies
Week 1Single-agent tool for your teamDay 1 + Langfuse from day one
Week 2–3Add memory to your agentDay 2.2 episodic + semantic
Month 1Scale to multi-agent (only if needed)Day 3.1 topology patterns
Month 2Evaluate and harden — 80% pass rateDay 3.2 eval harness
Month 3Optimise for cost + latency in productionDay 3.4 prompt caching + model routing
